reasoning: The Lean formalization captures exactly the informal claim. `IsNilpotent` for an ideal `I` unfolds to `∃ n, I^n = 0`, which in any nontrivial ring forces n ≥ 1 since `I^0 = ⊤` — equivalent to the informal "N^n = 0 for some n ≥ 1" (and in the trivial ring case `m=0`, the statement is still trivially true, so nothing is lost). `ZMod (p^m)` is the ring `ℤ/p^m ℤ`, and `span ({↑p} : Set (ZMod (p^m)))` is precisely the ideal $p\mathbb{Z}/p^m\mathbb{Z}$ generated by the image of `p`. The primality hypothesis on `p` is preserved, and no extraneous hypotheses (e.g., `m ≥ 1`) have been imposed. Thus the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal one.
